doc_findings:
Lungs and pleura: Pleuroapical scarring bilaterally. In the lingular segments of both lungs, discoid atelectasis and traction bronchiectasis. Bronchial walls are irregularly thickened; bronchial contents in some segmental bronchi. Mild centrilobular emphysema in both lungs. Small nodules in both lungs with a "tree-in-bud" pattern. Micronodules in both lungs measuring up to 3 mm in diameter. Small ground-glass opacities in the left lower lobe.  
Pulmonary nodules in series 5 are as follows:  
Right upper lobe:  
- Part-solid nodule measuring 5 mm on image 86.  
No pleural effusion.

Mediastinum and pulmonary hila: No mass or lymphadenopathy.

Heart: No cardiomegaly. No significant pericardial effusion. Incidental coronary artery calcification is present, qualitatively assessed as mild. The patient is advised to consult with their primary care physician for a comprehensive cardiovascular risk assessment or quantitative ordinal assessment of coronary artery calcification for each of the four main coronary arteries. Note: Recommendation applies unless the patient has known CAD, prior CABG/stent, trauma indications or intraoperative CTs. Aortic valve calcification. Pacemaker implantation.

Great vessels: Thoracic aorta is not dilated. Calcified atherosclerotic plaques in the wall of the thoracic aorta. C-shaped tortuosity of the thoracic aorta. Main pulmonary artery is not dilated.

Skeletal structures and chest wall: Degenerative changes of the thoracic spine. Hardware in the C6 vertebral body.

Upper abdomen: No abnormality.
doc_impression:
1. Numerous bilateral pulmonary nodules, with the largest in the left lower lobe, part-solid, measuring up to 5 mm, with progression compared with April 10, 2025 and October 07, 2025. Per 2017 Fleischner Society recommendations, chest CT in 12 months.

2. CT findings of left lower lobe bronchopneumonia.

3. CT findings of bronchiolitis.

4. Bronchiectasis in both lungs. Discoid atelectasis in both lungs.

5. Mild pulmonary emphysema. Pulmonary emphysema on CT is an independent risk factor for lung cancer. Consider enrollment in a low-dose CT (LDCT) lung cancer screening program if the patient meets eligibility criteria; today's CT may serve as a baseline for future screening. Note: Recommendation applies unless the patient has a history of lung cancer, known pulmonary nodules or lung mass, receives routine chest CTs/screening, or is receiving palliative care.

6. Incidental coronary artery calcification is present, qualitatively assessed as mild. The patient is advised to consult with their primary care physician for a comprehensive cardiovascular risk assessment or quantitative ordinal assessment of coronary artery calcification for each of the four main coronary arteries. Note: Recommendation applies unless the patient has known CAD, prior CABG/stent, trauma indications or intraoperative CTs. Aortic valve calcification. Pacemaker implantation.
val_findings:
Lungs and pleura: Biapical pleural-parenchymal scarring, small areas of paraseptal emphysema. There is persistent consolidation involving the middle lobe and lingula. Multiple tree-in-bud nodules are scattered throughout both lungs, compared to Chest CT on October 7, new centrilobular areas of ground-glass opacity, predominantly in the lower left lobe, for example, on images 138, 175. Segmental bronchi with thickened walls. Subsegmental bronchi with thickened walls, large volumes of mucus, and the formation of bronchiectasis and bronchocele. 
Pulmonary nodules in series 5 are as follows:  
Right upper lobe:  
- New part-solid nodule measures 5 mm on image 86, likely centrilobular, inflammatory. 
No pleural effusion.

Mediastinum and pulmonary hila: No mass or lymphadenopathy.

Heart: No cardiomegaly. Enlargement of the left ventricle. No significant pericardial effusion. Coronary artery calcifications are identified in the left main, left anterior descending, left circumflex arteries, qualitatively assessed as mild. Aortic valve calcifications. Pacemaker. 

Great vessels: Thoracic aorta is not dilated. Calcified atherosclerotic plaques in the wall of the thoracic aorta. C-shaped tortuosity of the thoracic aorta. Main pulmonary artery is not dilated.

Skeletal structures and chest wall: Degenerative changes of the thoracic spine. Healing fracture of the 6th rib on the right. Haller index 3.4 (pectus excavatum). 

Upper abdomen: No abnormality.
val_impression:
1. Bilateral consolidation, multiple tree-in-bud nodules, bronchial wall thickening with mucous plugging and bronchiectasis predominantly involving the right middle lobe and lingula consistent with atypical mycobacterial infection (MAC/MAI). Interval worsening compared to CT on October 7, 2025, due to the appearance of new centrilobular ground-glass nodules. 
New part-solid nodule measures 5 mm in the right upper lobe, likely centrilobular, inflammatory. ACR Fleischner Society recommendations (MacMahon, et al. Radiology 2017; 284(1): 228-43) suggest the following: No need for routine follow-up. However, if the nodule has suspicious morphology or if the patient belongs to a higher-risk population group, consider follow-up chest CT at 2 and 4 years.

2. Mild paraseptal pulmonary emphysema. Pulmonary emphysema on CT is an independent risk factor for lung cancer. Consider enrollment in a low-dose CT (LDCT) lung cancer screening program if the patient meets eligibility criteria; today's CT may serve as a baseline for future screening. Note: Recommendation applies unless the patient has a history of lung cancer, known pulmonary nodules or lung mass, receives routine chest CTs/screening, or is receiving palliative care.

3. Incidental coronary artery calcification is present, qualitatively assessed as mild. The patient is advised to consult with their primary care physician for a comprehensive cardiovascular risk assessment or quantitative ordinal assessment of coronary artery calcification for each of the four main coronary arteries. Note: Recommendation applies unless the patient has known CAD, prior CABG/stent, trauma indications or intraoperative CTs. Aortic valve calcification. Enlargement of the left ventricle. 


DISCLAIMER: AI lung nodule detection was utilized on this study. This system is designed for high sensitivity, potentially identifying benign findings. Please refer to the radiologist's interpretation to assess the clinical significance of any detected findings.
val_protocol:
INDICATION: Bronchiectasis with an acute lower respiratory infection. Cough for years.

COMPARISON: Chest CT on October 7, 2025, April 10, 2025.

TECHNIQUE: Scans were obtained through the thorax without IV contrast, to include contiguous axial images with bone and soft tissue algorithm. Thin slice axial reformat and coronal reformat images were included. Artificial Intelligence assessment was performed separately on the Nodule Prediction workstation. CT scan done according to ALARA (As Low As Reasonably Achievable).

RADIATION DOSE: CTDI volume6.49 mGy; total DLP 258.67 mGy-cm.

Prior known CT or cardiac nuclear medicine studies performed in the last 12 months: 3.

doc_findings:
FINDINGS

Lungs and pleura: The study is performed with respiratory motion artifacts.  
Areas of paraseptal emphysema in the lungs.  
A few solid nodules are present in the lung parenchyma, in series 5 as follows:  
- In the right lung:  
  - In the upper lobe:  
    - Micronodule measuring up to 2 mm (image 71).  
    - Solid nodule measuring up to 3 mm (image 148).  
- In the left lung:  
  - In the lower lobe:  
    - Solid nodule measuring up to 9 mm (image 108).  
Right pleural effusion with layer thickness up to 1.3 cm, with mild compression of the posterior basal lung parenchyma. Linear atelectasis is also present in the right middle lobe and left basal segments.  
No pleural effusion on the left.  
The trachea and central bronchi are patent.

Mediastinum and pulmonary hila: No mass or lymphadenopathy.

Heart: No cardiomegaly. No significant pericardial effusion.  
Severe coronary calcification (extensive plaques in the left anterior descending and circumflex arteries; hyperdense linear inclusions in the right coronary artery region—status post stenting: correlate with clinical history).

Great vessels:  
The thoracic aorta is not dilated, with wall calcification.  
The main pulmonary artery is dilated up to 3.0 cm.

Skeletal structures and chest wall: Minimal degenerative changes of the thoracic spine.  
Osteosclerotic lesions in the bones (in the right humeral head) up to 5 mm.

Upper abdomen: Unremarkable.

Thyroid gland: Hypodense lesion in the right lobe up to 1.0 × 0.9 cm.

Soft tissues at the level of the examination: Breast implants. Their contours are sharp, focally irregular, with calcification on the left; no localized fluid collections along the implants are identified.


IMPRESSION

1. CT findings of a solid nodule in the left lower lobe. Per Fleischner Society recommendations, consider CT in 3 months, PET/CT, or tissue sampling. A few small solid nodules in the right lung. Pulmonary emphysema. Small right pleural effusion with compressive changes in the posterior basal right lung.

2. Dilated main pulmonary artery (3.0 cm), which may suggest pulmonary hypertension.

3. Severe coronary calcification; hyperdense linear inclusions in the right coronary artery (status post stenting?: correlate with clinical history).  
Incidental coronary artery calcification is present, qualitatively assessed as severe.  
The patient is advised to consult with their primary care physician for a comprehensive cardiovascular risk assessment or quantitative ordinal assessment of coronary artery calcification for each of the four main coronary arteries.  
Note: Recommendation applies unless the patient has known CAD, prior CABG/stent, trauma indications or intraoperative CTs.

4. Atherosclerosis of the thoracic aorta.

5. Minimal degenerative changes of the thoracic spine with osteophyte formation. A few osteosclerotic lesions in the bones (consistent with enostoses) - Bone-RADS 1.

6. Hypodense lesion in the right thyroid lobe: per “Managing Incidental Thyroid Nodules Detected on Imaging: White Paper of the ACR Incidental Thyroid Findings Committee 2014,” no additional evaluation is required.

doc_findings:
FINDINGS:

Lungs and pleura: Pulmonary nodules in series 4 are as follows:
Right upper lobe:
- Faint ground-glass nodule measuring 6 mm on image 61.
Right middle lobe:
- Solid subpleural nodule measuring 3 mm on image 200.
Right lower lobe:
- Solid subpleural nodule measuring 3 mm on image 216.
Left upper lobe:
- Solid subpleural nodule measuring 3 mm on image 101.
- Solid subpleural nodule measuring 3 mm on image 145.
In both lungs, solid micronodules measure up to 2 mm.
In the right upper and middle lobes, there are areas of consolidation and ground-glass opacity, predominantly linear in configuration, series 4, images 113-193.
In the right upper and middle lobes (at the level of the above-described areas of increased lung opacity), lumens of dilated bronchi are present.
In both lungs, there are a few cysts, the largest in the right upper lobe measuring up to 1.9 × 1.3 cm, series 4, images 44-52.
In the right middle lobe, there is a calcified granuloma measuring up to 3 mm.
No pleural effusion.

Mediastinum and pulmonary hila: No mass or lymphadenopathy.

Heart: No cardiomegaly.
Moderate pericardial effusion measuring up to 1 cm.
No coronary calcification.

Great vessels: Thoracic aorta is not dilated, mild wall calcification.
Main pulmonary artery is not dilated.

Skeletal structures and chest wall: Moderate degenerative changes of the thoracic spine.
Decreased height of the T7 and T8 vertebral bodies by 15% and 10%, respectively.

Upper abdomen: In the left kidney, there are two adjacent rounded lesions (or one "figure-8" lesion) with density up to 9 HU, measuring up to 2.6 × 2.8 cm and 2.8 × 2.5 cm.
doc_impression:
1. Solid micronodules in both lungs and solid nodules in the right middle and lower lobes and left upper lobe: per 2017 Fleischner Society guidelines, in high-risk patients, follow-up chest CT in 12 months; in low-risk patients, no follow-up is required.
Ground-glass nodule in the right upper lobe: per 2017 Fleischner Society guidelines, CT at 6–12 months to confirm persistence, then CT every 2 years until 5 years.
Calcified granuloma in the right middle lobe, consistent with prior infection.
A few pulmonary cysts.

2. Previously reported opacities in the right lung on the chest radiograph dated February 26, 2026 correspond on the current study to areas of consolidation and ground-glass opacity, more likely representing sequelae of prior multilobar pneumonia.
Bronchiectasis in the right upper and middle lobes.

3. Moderate pericardial effusion.

4. Mild atherosclerosis of the thoracic aorta.

5. Moderate degenerative changes of the thoracic spine.
Mild decrease in height of the T7 and T8 vertebral bodies; no further evaluation is required.

6. Hypodense left renal lesions: per Management of the Incidental Renal Mass on CT: A White Paper of the ACR Incidental Findings Committee, 2018, no further evaluation is required.

A few cysts are present in both lungs, the largest in the right upper lobe measuring up to 1.9 × 1.3 cm (series 4, images 44–52).
Calcified granuloma in the right middle lobe measures up to 3 mm.

val_impression:
1. Solid nodules in both lungs and ground-glass nodule in the right upper lobe.  ACR Fleischner Society recommendations (MacMahon, et al. Radiology 2017; 284(1): 228-43) suggest the following: For patients with low risk of lung cancer, no need for further follow-up. For patients with high risk of lung cancer, generally no need for follow-up. An optional follow-up chest CT at 12 months could be performed if there is high index of suspicion. If unchanged, no further follow-up is necessary.

2. A few bilateral pulmonary cysts.

3. Previously reported opacities in the right lung on the chest radiograph dated February 26, 2026 correspond on the current study with areas of atelectasis and ground-glass opacity, most likely representing sequelae of prior multilobar pneumonia.

4. Moderate pericardial effusion.

5. Mild atherosclerosis of the thoracic aorta.

6. Moderate degenerative changes of the thoracic spine.

7. Hypodense left renal lesions. ACR White Paper recommendations (Herts, et al. J Am Coll Radiol 2018;15:264-273) suggest the following: Likely benign cyst. No need for further work-up.

DISCLAIMER: AI lung nodule detection was utilized on this study. This system is designed for high sensitivity, potentially identifying benign findings. Please refer to the radiologist's interpretation to assess the clinical significance of any detected findings.

doc_impression:
1. Multiple tree-in-bud nodules and ground-glass opacities, consistent with respiratory bronchiolitis, unchanged compared with the CT of the abdomen including the lung bases from April 02, 2026.

2. Solid small pulmonary nodule; considering risk factors, optional follow-up at 12 months per the Updated Fleischner Society guideline 2017.

3. Incidental coronary artery calcification is present, qualitatively assessed as mild. The patient is advised to consult with their primary care physician for a comprehensive cardiovascular risk assessment or quantitative ordinal assessment of coronary artery calcification for each of the four main coronary arteries.

4. A few large renal cysts, likely benign. No further follow-up is required.

5. Aortic calcification and mild aortic valve calcification.

6. Severe degenerative changes of the thoracic spine with increased kyphosis. Osteoarthritis of the shoulder joints.

COMPARISON: Abdomen CT on 04/06/26.

TECHNIQUE: Scans were obtained through the thorax without IV contrast, to include contiguous axial images with bone and soft tissue algorithm. Thin slice axial reformat and coronal reformat images were included. Artificial Intelligence assessment was performed separately on the Nodule Prediction workstation. Prior known CT or cardiac nuclear medicine studies performed in the last 12 months: 1.

doc_findings:
Lungs and pleura: Solid micronodules up to 2 mm in both lungs (no interval change), calcified granulomas up to 5 mm (no interval change), and discoid atelectasis. No pleural effusion.

Mediastinum and pulmonary hila: No mass or lymphadenopathy. Calcifications in right hilar lymph nodes. Hiatal hernia with herniation of part of the stomach into the lower posterior mediastinum, measuring 5.9 × 6.3 cm.

Heart: No cardiomegaly. No significant pericardial effusion. Coronary calcification in the left main, left anterior descending, left circumflex, and right coronary arteries, qualitatively assessed as severe. Calcification of the aortic and mitral valves.

Great vessels: Thoracic aorta is not dilated, with severe wall calcification. Main pulmonary artery is not dilated.

Skeletal structures and chest wall: Severe degenerative changes of the thoracic spine. Retrolisthesis of L1 and L2 by 2 mm (Meyerding grade 1). Bone demineralization. Healing fractures of the left 5th and 6th ribs.

Upper abdomen: Cholecystectomy. In the upper pole of the right kidney, a lesion with density 0 HU, measuring about 1.0 cm, with indistinct margins (no change in size compared with CT chest dated March 06, 2025). In the upper pole of the left kidney, a lesion with density 44 HU, measuring about 1.6 × 1.2 cm, with indistinct margins (no definite change in size compared with CT chest dated March 06, 2025 and October 25, 2025).
doc_impression:
1. Multiple stable micronodules in both lungs. Fleischner Society recommendations may not be applicable considering the clinical history. Follow-up per oncology follow-up protocol. Discoid atelectasis in both lungs. Calcified granulomas in both lungs and calcifications in intrathoracic lymph nodes are consistent with prior infection.

2. Coronary calcification, qualitatively assessed as severe. The patient is advised to consult with their primary care physician for consideration of preventive risk modification or quantitative ordinal assessment of coronary artery calcification for each of the four main coronary arteries. Calcification of the aortic and mitral valves.

3. Severe atherosclerosis of the thoracic aorta.

4. Hiatal hernia.

5. Stable right renal lesion; per Management of the Incidental Renal Mass on CT: A White Paper of the ACR Incidental Findings Committee, 2018, no further evaluation is required. Stable left renal lesion; per Management of the Incidental Renal Mass on CT: A White Paper of the ACR Incidental Findings Committee, 2018, no further evaluation is required.

6. Severe degenerative changes of the thoracic spine. Retrolisthesis of L1 and L2 (Meyerding grade 1). Bone demineralization. Healing fractures of the left 5th and 6th ribs.

7. Cholecystectomy.
val_findings:
Lungs and pleura: In the lung bases, bilateral gravitational changes, discoid atelectasis, and interstitial changes. Multiple calcified granulomas up to 5 mm (no interval change). A few solid micronodules up to 2 mm in both lungs (no interval change). No pleural effusion.

Mediastinum and pulmonary hila: No mass or lymphadenopathy. Calcifications in hilar lymph nodes. Hiatal hernia with herniation of part of the stomach into the lower posterior mediastinum, measuring 5.9 × 6.3 cm.

Heart: No cardiomegaly. No significant pericardial effusion. Coronary calcification in the left main, left anterior descending, left circumflex, and right coronary arteries, qualitatively assessed as severe. Calcification of the aortic and mitral valves.

Great vessels: Thoracic aorta is not dilated, with severe wall calcification. Main pulmonary artery is not dilated.

Skeletal structures and chest wall: Severe degenerative changes of the thoracic spine. Retrolisthesis of L1 and L2 by 3 mm (Meyerding grade 1), pronounced erosive changes in the endplates of L2 and L3, unchanged. Bone demineralization, with decreased vertebral body density to 50 HU. Healing fractures of the left 5th and 6th ribs.

Upper abdomen: Please see separately dictated abdomen CT, performed concurrently.
val_impression:
1. A few stable micronodules in both lungs. Fleischner Society recommendations may not be applicable considering the clinical history. Follow-up per oncology follow-up protocol.

2. Calcified granulomas in both lungs and calcifications in intrathoracic lymph nodes are consistent with prior infection and require no follow-up.

3. Coronary calcification, qualitatively assessed as severe. The patient is advised to consult with their primary care physician for consideration of preventive risk modification or quantitative ordinal assessment of coronary artery calcification for each of the four main coronary arteries. Note: Recommendation applies unless the patient has a history of lung cancer, known pulmonary nodules or lung mass, receives routine chest CTs/screening, or is receiving palliative care.

4. Severe atherosclerosis of the thoracic aorta. Calcification of the aortic and mitral valves.

5. Hiatal hernia.

6. Retrolisthesis of L1 and L2 (Meyerding grade 1). Bone demineralization. 


DISCLAIMER: AI lung nodule detection was utilized on this study. This system is designed for high sensitivity, potentially identifying benign findings. Please refer to the radiologist's interpretation to assess the clinical significance of any detected findings.
